pattern: The topmost value after exactly k moves can only come from one of two shapes: pop straight down to depth k and stop, or pop down to depth k-1 and place the best of what you've already lifted back on top - so the answer collapses to comparing those two candidates.
hints: A pile of size one is a special trap: popping it empties the pile, so the parity of k alone decides whether anything is left on top at the end.

Every other final top is either the element sitting exactly k positions down (if you just keep popping), or the best value among the first k-1 elements (if you pop that many and then place the best one back with your last move).

Compute the running maximum of the first k-1 elements and separately look at the element at index k (if it exists); the answer is the larger of the two, after handling the single-element pile as its own case.

edge_inputs.description: typical case where popping past a bigger buried value pays off
single element pile, one move empties it
k = 1 forces exactly one pop with no chance to place anything back, so only the second element is visible
k equal to pile length: still only the k-1 popped elements can be replaced on top, the last pop's reveal is the other option
k greater than pile length, so the whole array is available to place back but the direct-reveal option no longer exists
zeros mixed in so max must not default to a falsy check
single element pile with an even k, pile survives

stress.description: 10000-element pile with k ranging across the small-k, mid-k, and k>=n regimes
stress.note: covers k=0, k=1, k==n, k>n and the general mid-range so no branch of the case analysis goes untested at scale
